Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around whatever time I could find between my part-time work and other responsibilities. I'd usually set aside late evenings and weekends for studying, aiming for about 3-4 hours daily when possible. I kept a simple notebook where I'd write down what topics I needed to cover each week. The plan was solid on paper, but honestly, life kept throwing curveballs that made sticking to it really difficult.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life. When deadlines were approaching, I'd go into overdrive and study intensely for days. But during calmer weeks, I tried to maintain some regularity, studying after dinner most nights. The problem was that my work schedule wasn't consistent, so sometimes I'd have to shift everything around at the last minute.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check deadlines or download lecture materials. Sometimes I'd get notifications about new content or discussion posts, and that would prompt me to log in. I also logged in frequently just to see if there were any announcements I might have missed because I was scared of falling behind without realizing it. The fear of missing something important kept me checking regularly.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning and again at night, almost like checking my phone for messages. It happened because I realized early on that the course moved fast and I couldn't afford to miss updates. The gamified tasks they introduced actually made me want to check in more often to see my progress.
Q5: The reading materials and written notes were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download the PDFs and annotate them heavily with my own notes. The discussion forums were also valuable because I could see how other students interpreted difficult concepts. When the gamified tasks were introduced, the progress tracking helped me see what I'd accomplished, which felt motivating even when things were tough.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only watched them partially. With limited data and internet that wasn't always reliable, streaming long videos was frustrating and expensive. I figured I could get the same information from the written materials anyway. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also delayed because I was always prioritizing the graded work first.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the exams or assignments. If I understood how something would help me pass, I was motivated to engage with it. The gamified elements helped too because earning points or badges made even routine tasks feel more rewarding. I also valued activities where I could learn something practical that I might use later.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize required work first. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting and I'd feel guilty for not engaging with it more. The required activities carried this weight of anxiety because failing them meant failing the course, while optional ones felt like pressure-free exploration when I could manage it.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the PDFs and readings to my phone or laptop and study them without internet. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and the library since I couldn't afford to buy them all. A lot of my learning came from handwritten notes I made while reading, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or working through problems in a notebook, completely disconnected from the LMS. I'd also form small study groups with classmates where we'd meet in person to discuss difficult topics. Sometimes I'd re-read the same material multiple times offline to really understand it, but the LMS would only show that I downloaded it once.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then study them extensively offline over several days or weeks. My internet wasn't always reliable, and I needed to save data, so working offline just made more sense. I'd return to the same downloaded PDF multiple times, making new annotations each time as my understanding deepened.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming. When exam season hit and I was also working extra shifts to pay bills, engaging with the course became really hard. I'd feel scared that I was falling too far behind to catch up. My stress levels would spike and I'd sometimes freeze instead of being productive, just staring at the materials without absorbing anything.
Q13: Competing responsibilities affected me constantly. When work demanded more hours, my LMS activity would drop significantly. Fatigue was a real issue too – after a long shift, I'd log in with good intentions but find myself too exhausted to engage meaningfully. Confusion about certain topics also slowed me down because I'd spend time trying to understand basics instead of moving forward with new material.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be worried about money or exhausted from work, and even though I was technically accessing content, nothing was sticking. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I'd absorbed almost nothing because my thoughts kept drifting to other problems.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was fear – I was scared of starting something and realizing I didn't understand it well enough. Sometimes I'd tell myself I needed to review the material more before attempting the assignment, and that reviewing would stretch on too long. My low assignment completion rate reflects this pattern of avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and terrifying for me. They'd push me to finally tackle things I'd been avoiding, but the panic mode wasn't great for quality work. I'd often log in much more frequently as deadlines approached, sometimes staying up late to submit things. The problem was that this deadline-driven approach meant I wasn't engaging consistently throughout the course.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, though I didn't always get it quickly enough. When I received positive feedback on my assignment work, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and sometimes by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. I wish there had been more immediate feedback built into the learning process.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. The pace was fast and assumed consistent engagement, which was hard when my life was unpredictable. However, when they introduced the gamified tasks, it helped break things into smaller, more manageable pieces. I appreciated having clear milestones, though I struggled when multiple deadlines clustered together.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier because they gave me small wins to celebrate even when the bigger picture felt overwhelming. The discussion forums were accessible and didn't require much data to use. What made it harder was the heavy reliance on video content and the assumption that everyone had consistent internet access. The pacing also didn't account for students who needed more time to grasp concepts.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly active in the LMS – logging in frequently, participating in discussions, engaging with the gamified tasks – but my actual assignment submission rate was low. The fear of not doing well enough on assignments made me avoid submitting them, even when I'd done the work. Sometimes being active is about trying to learn and understand, while submitting work requires a different kind of confidence.
Q21: That could happen if someone is really efficient with their time or learns well from external resources. Some students might download everything once and master it offline. I tried to do this but my exam performance shows I wasn't entirely successful. Still, I think LMS activity and actual learning aren't perfectly correlated – someone could be deeply engaged through methods that don't show up in the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, and basic participation metrics accurately. They capture when you're present in the system. But they miss the quality of engagement – whether you're actually understanding material or just scrolling through it. They can't see the offline studying, the mental effort, or the personal struggles that affect how you process information. They show the what but not the why.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to explore it deeper, when I'm actively thinking about how concepts connect. For me, engagement also means persisting even when things get difficult, which I tried to do despite my circumstances. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just physical presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the fear I felt constantly about failing, or the resilience it took to keep logging in even when I was exhausted and scared. It can't see the hours I spent studying from borrowed books or handwritten notes, or the stress of balancing work and studies. My engagement was intense, even if my results don't fully reflect that.
